meta.title: TermRender — Terminal Rendering Engine
meta.subtitle: EARS Behavioral Spec
meta.summary: A standalone Python terminal rendering library that takes directive-flavored markdown as input and outputs rich ANSI-styled terminal output with panels, columns, trees, and mermaid flowcharts. Optimized for token-efficient LLM agent output.

groups.name: Input Format
groups.description: The markup language agents write to produce rendered output
groups.context:
The input format must balance token efficiency (agents pay per token) with expressiveness. Markdown is the base because LLMs already speak it fluently. Container directives (:::) add layout semantics. Mermaid code blocks add diagrams. All styling goes through directives — no passthrough ANSI.

  Agent writes:          Parser produces:         Renderer outputs:
  ┌──────────┐          ┌──────────┐             ┌──────────┐
  │ Markdown │ ──parse──► │   AST    │ ──layout──► │  ANSI    │
  │ + :::    │          │  (tree)  │  + render   │  string  │
  │ + mmd    │          │          │             │          │
  └──────────┘          └──────────┘             └──────────┘

requirements.title: Markdown base format

ears.shall: the system shall accept standard CommonMark markdown as the base content format, rendering headings, paragraphs, bold, italic, code spans, code blocks, and lists

criteria.text: Headings (h1-h4) render with appropriate styling/emphasis
Inline formatting (bold, italic, code) maps to ANSI attributes
Code blocks render with borders or indentation
Unordered and ordered lists render correctly

requirements.agentNotes: CommonMark is the safest base — it's well-specified and every LLM produces it natively. We avoid GFM extensions (tables, strikethrough) initially to keep the parser simple, but the AST should be extensible.

questions.question: Should we support GFM tables in v1, or defer?

requirements.title: Container directives for layout

ears.when: When the input contains ::: directive blocks
ears.shall: the system shall parse them as layout containers with optional attributes in {key=value} syntax, supporting: panel, columns/col, tree, callout, quote, code, and divider

criteria.text: :::panel{title="X" color="green"} creates a bordered panel
:::columns with nested :::col blocks creates side-by-side layout
:::tree renders indented content as a tree with guide lines
:::callout{type="warning"} renders a colored callout box (info/warning/error/success)
:::quote{by="Author"} renders a styled blockquote with attribution
:::code{lang="python"} renders a syntax-highlighted code block with panel chrome
:::divider{label="Section"} renders a horizontal rule with optional centered label
Unknown directive names are treated as plain panels (graceful fallback)

requirements.agentNotes: Revised per review — expanded from 3 directive types to 7. The ::: syntax is from the generic directives proposal (MyST, remark-directive). Directives are purely semantic — they carry no visual information except hints like color/type. callout, quote, code, and divider are the most commonly needed beyond the structural panel/columns/tree.
requirements.userNotes: User asked: 'Are there others we should support?' — added callout, quote, code, divider.

questions.question: Are there any additional directive types beyond these 7 that you want in v1?

requirements.title: Mermaid code blocks

ears.when: When the input contains a fenced code block with language 'mermaid'
ears.shall: the system shall parse the mermaid syntax and render it as an ASCII/Unicode flowchart inline with surrounding content, using mermaid-ascii as the rendering backend

criteria.text: graph TD (top-down) layout is supported
graph LR (left-right) layout is supported
Node labels with [] syntax are rendered
Edge labels with |text| syntax are rendered
Branching (A --> B & C) is supported
mermaid-ascii is used as a Python dependency, not shelled out to

requirements.agentNotes: Revised per review — since this is a Python package, we use mermaid-ascii directly as a library dependency. No subprocess overhead, no Python runtime concern. Sequence diagram support depends on what mermaid-ascii supports.
requirements.userNotes: User: 'We're going to make this a python package, so it's okay if it has python deps'

requirements.title: Token efficiency

ears.while: While the input format is being designed
ears.shall: the system shall ensure that layout and styling are expressed through semantic directives, never requiring the agent to output decorative characters (borders, box-drawing, padding, repeated symbols)

criteria.text: A full-featured panel with title, border, and color costs <10 tokens of directive overhead
Multi-column layout requires only :::columns/:::col markers, not manual spacing
Trees use indentation only, not manual guide-line characters
All styling goes through directives — no raw ANSI passthrough

requirements.agentNotes: This is the primary motivation for the project. Rich-style output requires agents to manually draw borders and pad content, wasting 200+ tokens per panel. Directive syntax encodes the same information in ~10 tokens. Directives-only policy confirmed in review (no ANSI passthrough).

groups.name: Layout Engine
groups.description: How the system computes sizes, positions, and composes nested blocks
groups.context:
The layout engine is the hardest part. It needs to handle arbitrary nesting of panels, columns, trees, and diagrams. Think CSS block model but dramatically simpler — only vertical stacking, horizontal columns, and padding/borders. Content that exceeds available width is hard-wrapped at the container boundary.

  ┌─ Panel ────────────────────────┐
  │ ┌─ Columns ──────────────────┐ │
  │ │ ┌─ Col 1 ─┐ ┌─ Col 2 ────┐│ │
  │ │ │ text    │ │ :::tree    ││ │
  │ │ │         │ │   src/     ││ │
  │ │ │         │ │     foo.ts ││ │
  │ │ └─────────┘ └────────────┘│ │
  │ └────────────────────────────┘ │
  │ ```mermaid                     │
  │ ┌───┐    ┌───┐    ┌───┐       │
  │ │ A │───►│ B │───►│ C │       │
  │ └───┘    └───┘    └───┘       │
  └────────────────────────────────┘

requirements.title: Arbitrary nesting

ears.when: When directive blocks are nested inside each other
ears.shall: the system shall correctly compute layout for arbitrarily nested combinations of panels, columns, trees, and mermaid diagrams

criteria.text: Panels can contain columns, trees, mermaid blocks, and other panels
Columns can contain panels, trees, and mermaid blocks
Layout constraints propagate inward (parent width constrains children)
Minimum width is respected — content is never clipped below its minimum renderable width

requirements.agentNotes: Arbitrary nesting makes the layout engine recursive. Each node computes its intrinsic size, then the parent allocates available width. This is a simplified constraint solver — not full CSS flexbox, but the same idea directionally.

requirements.title: Terminal width adaptation

ears.shall: the system shall detect the terminal width and adapt all layout to fit, wrapping text content and scaling columns proportionally

criteria.text: Default to stdout terminal width (shutil.get_terminal_size())
Accept explicit width override for testing/piping
Text wraps at word boundaries within containers
Columns distribute available width proportionally

requirements.agentNotes: Word wrapping inside nested containers is tricky — available width is terminal width minus all ancestor padding/borders. Need to track 'available width' as we recurse down the tree.

requirements.title: Column layout

ears.when: When a :::columns block contains multiple :::col children
ears.shall: the system shall render them side-by-side, distributing available width equally by default or according to explicit width attributes

criteria.text: Default: equal width split among columns
Explicit width: :::col{width=60%} or :::col{width=30}
Columns are padded to equal height with empty lines
Column separator (single-char vertical line or gap) between columns

requirements.agentNotes: Columns are rendered independently to string buffers, then zipped line-by-line. Each column is rendered at its allocated width, split into lines, padded to max height, then joined horizontally.

questions.question: Should columns have visible separators (│) or just whitespace gaps?

requirements.title: Overflow handling

ears.when: When content exceeds available width after all nesting is accounted for
ears.shall: the system shall hard-wrap the content at the container boundary

criteria.text: Text wraps at word boundaries when possible
Long unbreakable tokens (URLs, code) wrap mid-token at the boundary
Mermaid diagrams that exceed available width are rendered at their natural size and clipped

requirements.agentNotes: Hard wrap was chosen over overflow indicators or stacking fallback. Simplest behavior, easiest to reason about. Confirmed in review.

groups.name: Styling & Colors
groups.description: How colors and visual attributes are specified and rendered

requirements.title: Directive-based color

ears.when: When a directive includes a color attribute
ears.shall: the system shall apply that color to the container's border and title using ANSI color codes

criteria.text: Named colors: red, green, blue, yellow, magenta, cyan, white, gray
Color applies to borders and title, not content text by default
Nested containers can have different colors

requirements.agentNotes: Keep the color model simple — named colors only, no hex/RGB. 8-color ANSI works everywhere. 256-color/truecolor could be a future extension.

requirements.title: Panel styling

ears.shall: the system shall draw a Unicode box border around the content with optional title, padding, and color

criteria.text: Title renders centered or left-aligned in the top border
1 character padding inside the panel by default
Border uses box-drawing characters (─│┌┐└┘)

requirements.agentNotes: Panel = border + padding + title. Single-line Unicode box-drawing only. No ASCII fallback — see REQ-011.

questions.question: Should panels support different border styles (single, double, rounded, heavy) or just one?

requirements.title: Unicode-only rendering

ears.if: If the terminal does not support Unicode or ANSI colors
ears.shall: the system shall raise an error and refuse to render, rather than falling back to degraded output

criteria.text: Raises a clear error if TERM=dumb or terminal lacks Unicode support
No ASCII fallback code path — single rendering implementation
NO_COLOR environment variable disables color but still requires Unicode support

requirements.agentNotes: Revised per review — user explicitly requested throwing instead of degrading. 'We don't want to support two systems in one.' This simplifies the codebase significantly: one rendering path, no conditional box-drawing. NO_COLOR is still respected since it's about color only, not character set.
requirements.userNotes: User: 'It should throw. We don't want to support two systems in one'

groups.name: API & Packaging
groups.description: How the library is consumed — CLI, programmatic API, and distribution

requirements.title: CLI interface

ears.when: When invoked as a CLI tool
ears.shall: the system shall accept input from a file argument or stdin and write rendered output to stdout

criteria.text: termrender file.md renders the file
cat file.md | termrender renders from stdin
Supports --width and --no-color flags

requirements.agentNotes: Simple Unix-philosophy interface. Pipe-friendly. Removed --ascii flag since we no longer support ASCII fallback.

questions.question: What should the CLI command name be?

requirements.title: Programmatic API

ears.when: When imported as a Python library
ears.shall: the system shall expose a render(input: str, width: int | None = None, color: bool = True) -> str function that returns the ANSI-styled output

criteria.text: Single-function API: render(markdown) → str
Options for width and color mode
Type hints included (py.typed marker)
Importable as: from termrender import render

requirements.agentNotes: Revised for Python. Keep it dead simple — one function, one string in, one string out. Type hints for IDE support.

requirements.title: Standalone Python package

ears.shall: the system shall be a standalone Python package on PyPI, installable independently of sisyphus, with mermaid-ascii and a markdown parser as its primary dependencies

criteria.text: Published to PyPI as 'termrender'
pip install termrender installs CLI and library
Dependencies: mermaid-ascii, markdown parser (mistune or markdown-it-py), Rich (for ANSI primitives)
Works on Python 3.10+

requirements.agentNotes: Revised per review — Python package on PyPI instead of npm. mermaid-ascii is a direct dependency. Rich can be used for low-level ANSI output (colors, styles) without using its layout/panel system — we build our own layout engine on top.
requirements.userNotes: User: 'It can be python — it doesn't need to be ts'

questions.question: Should we use Rich as a dependency for ANSI primitives, or handle ANSI escape codes directly?

groups.name: Tree Rendering
groups.description: How tree structures are specified and displayed

requirements.title: Indentation-based tree input

ears.when: When a :::tree directive contains indented text lines
ears.shall: the system shall render them as a tree with Unicode guide lines (├── └── │) preserving the hierarchy from indentation depth

criteria.text: 2-space or 4-space indentation both work
Guide lines connect siblings and show hierarchy
Last child uses └── instead of ├──
Color attribute applies to guide lines

requirements.agentNotes: Trees from indentation is extremely token-efficient — the agent just indents naturally. No special syntax needed beyond the :::tree wrapper.

questions.question: Should trees also support markdown nested lists as input (- item / - subitem), or only raw indented text?

requirements.title: Tree node annotations

ears.where: Where a tree node includes inline markup
ears.shall: the system shall render bold, italic, color spans, and status indicators (checkmarks, crosses) within tree node labels

criteria.text: Bold/italic in node labels via markdown syntax
Status markers like [x] or [!] render as styled indicators
Node-level color override possible

requirements.agentNotes: Annotated trees are very useful for showing file trees with status (modified, added, deleted) or task trees with completion status.
